coredata.dc:title: Synergistic role of carbon quantum dots on biohydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sivagurunathan, Periyasamy
Sahoo, Prakash C.
Kumar, Manoj
Gupta, Ravi Prakash
Srivastva, Umish
Bhattacharyya, Debasis
coredata.dc:description: Biohybrid system has their distinct ability to improve microbial fermentation. This study demonstrates the role of surface-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on dark fermentative biohydrogen production using lactobacillus organic-hybrid biocatalyst. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with < 5 nm (having a surface charge of +2.6 mV) and un-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (having a surface charge of −4.5 mV) were synthesized via chemical assisted process. Subsequently, the biohybrid systems were constructed via augmentation of N-CQDs and CQDs with Lactobacillus delbreuckii and assessed for biohydrogen production. The results revealed that both the biohybrid systems (N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii and CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii) provided improved hydrogen production than that of the native bacterial strain. Interestingly, the obtained N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii system provided the maximum hydrogen yield of 2.01 mol/mol hexose, followed by 1.86 mol/mol hexose from CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii, which is about 33 % and 19 % higher than the bare bacterial strain. The electron transfer and metabolic alteration of microbes by carbon quantum dots were assessed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and VFA production. It was concluded that the improved bio-hydrogen production from N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii is attributed to enhanced electron transfer, which regulates the central metabolic pathway of acetate and butyrate synthesis with the least production of lactate and other reduced end product formation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biohydrogen
Biohybrid
Carbon quantum dots
Pathway inducer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, N-CQDs and its anchoring on Lactobacillus delbrueckii
Biohydrogen production from CQDs assisted biohybrid
Biohydrogen production from nitrogen doped N-QDs biohybrid
Proposed mechanism for the enhancement of hydrogen production by N- CQDs augmentation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface charge of the microbe and the synthesized CQDs.

TEM Image of carbon quantum dots (a) as synthesized N-CQDs (b) CQD (c) N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system (d) CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system. (e) Image of N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii under visible and (f) UV-light, (g) Image of Lactobacillus delbrecukii under UV-Light, and (h) Image of N-CQDs under UV-Light.
XRD analysis of a) CQDs and b) N-CQDs; PL spectrum analysis of c) CQDs and d) N-CQDs.
XPS analysis data of CQDs and N-CQDs.
IR spectra of CQDS and microbes a) LAB, b) LAB+ CQDs, c) LAB+ N-CQDS, d) CQDs, and e) N-CQDs.
CLSM images of the a) Lactobacillus delbrecukii, b) CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid CQDs and c) N-CQDs -Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid.
a) biohydrogen and b) volatile fatty acid production from CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system at varied concentrations.
a) biohydrogen and b) volatile fatty acid production from N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system at varied concentrations.
Hydrogenase gene activity of a) CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system and b) N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrecukii biohybrid system.
Electrochemical analysis of the bio hybrid system and bare bacteria, a) CV analysis, b) oxidation-reduction potential.
Kinetic parameters of biohydrogen production from CQDs biohybrid at varied concentration.
Kinetic parameters of biohydrogen production from N-QDs biohybrid at varied concentration.
Comparison of biohydrogen production with microbes and nanoparticles addition.

entry.italic: Megasphaera elsdenii co-culture with Lactobacillus delbrueckii

row.entry: About 17 % higher than Clostridium butyricum co-culture
About 25 % higher yield attained with magnetide supplementation
4 fold increment than the native strain without nonmaterial
18 % higher yield than the pure LAB
About two fold higher hydrogen production with catalyst and the presence of light 2000 W m−2

selenide/
sulfide semiconductor nanoparticles (CdSexS1-x)/ E.coli hybrid system
About 2.6 fold higher hydrogen production than extracellular nanoparticles
About 19 % higher yield than the Pure LAB
About 33 % higher yield than the Pure LAB

head.ce:title: Synergistic role of carbon quantum dots on biohydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Research & Development Centre, Sector 13, Faridabad, Haryana 121007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Research & Development Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Indian Oil Corporation Limited, Research & Development Centre, Sector 13, Faridabad 121007, Haryana, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Indian Oil Corporation, R&D Centre, Sector-13, Faridabad, Haryana 121007, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biohybrid system has their distinct ability to improve microbial fermentation. This study demonstrates the role of surface-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on dark fermentative biohydrogen production using lactobacillus organic-hybrid biocatalyst. Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with < 5 nm (having a surface charge of +2.6 mV) and un-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (having a surface charge of −4.5 mV) were synthesized via chemical assisted process. Subsequently, the biohybrid systems were constructed via augmentation of N-CQDs and CQDs with Lactobacillus delbreuckii and assessed for biohydrogen production. The results revealed that both the biohybrid systems (N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii and CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii) provided improved hydrogen production than that of the native bacterial strain. Interestingly, the obtained N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii system provided the maximum hydrogen yield of 2.01 mol/mol hexose, followed by 1.86 mol/mol hexose from CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii, which is about 33 % and 19 % higher than the bare bacterial strain. The electron transfer and metabolic alteration of microbes by carbon quantum dots were assessed using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and VFA production. It was concluded that the improved bio-hydrogen production from N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbreuckii is attributed to enhanced electron transfer, which regulates the central metabolic pathway of acetate and butyrate synthesis with the least production of lactate and other reduced end product formation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the current energy scenario, the demand and supply of the global energy requirement is met via non-conventional energy resources, which in turn increases the global warming potential and environmental pollution. Renewable biofuels show their potential to replace fossil-derived fuels and also offset the environmental challenges associated with the huge consumption of non-renewable energy sources [1]. In the context of various biofuel options such as biogas, bioethanol, and biobutanol, biohydrogen provides a higher heating value of 141.8 MJ/Kg than other fuels. Currently, various government organisations/policymakers and academic researchers across the globe working on the development of clean hydrogen production from non-renewable sources [2–4]. Hydrogen is the only sustainable fuel with carbon neutral ability during combustion and it is widely adopted by many industries like transportation, electricity generation, and fertilizer industry it acts as a major fuel for meeting the global net zero emissions in the upcoming years. Recently, the Indian Government also announced its green hydrogen mission and targeted to produce 5 MMT of hydrogen by the year 2030 [4]. Among various hydrogen production methods such as stem methane reforming, electrolysis, the biological assisted process is less energy intensive and can be operated at a moderate temperature range of 30–55 ºC. Biological mediated hydrogen production pathway includes biophotolysis by algae, photo fermentation by light-sensitive bacteria and dark fermentation by fermentative bacteria [5]. Hydrogen production via the dark-fermentation process is widely studied due to its renewable hydrogen production from various organic waste materials. In general, dark fermentative hydrogen production involves the fermentation of organic waste by a group of bacteria which includes, a pure culture of facultative anaerobic bacteria or strict anaerobes, and a consortium of microorganisms i.e mixed cultures. Despite, the merits of dark-fermentative hydrogen production from waste organics, several challenges, including low hydrogen yields and incomplete substrate utilization, limit the efficiency of dark fermentative biohydrogen production [6–8].

The metabolic activity of the hydrogen producers is greatly influenced by the augmentation of the nanomaterials along with the feedstock. Owing to the inherent characteristics of nanoparticles such as higher surface area, the difference in surface charge; electro-catalytic sites, electron transfer ability, and physicochemical interaction of nanoparticles with microbes provides an improvement in overall biohydrogen production ability [9–12]. As overviewed by previous studies, the quantum dots are a special category of nanomaterial i.e. semiconductors usually having a particle size < 10 nm possess a higher catalytic site for hydrogen production in biotic and abiotic conditions, under the influence of light interface as a source of energy donor. The interaction of quantum dots within the photo-assisted biohydrogen system demonstrated a greater increase in bacteria's biohydrogen production [11–14]. However, the light-dependent hydrogen production process is expensive in comparison to the traditional dark fermentation mode of hydrogen production., Further, dark fermentation is a predominant hydrogen production pathway for efficient hydrogen production from wastewater and organic waste, due to its better substrate degradation and higher production rates, however, the sluggish hydrogen yield limits the efficiency of the process, and the addition of CQDs may be proven to be the best case for improving dark fermentation system [2,15]. Adding CQDs could speed up hydrogen production by boosting hydrogenase activity, and soluble metabolic products synthesis. This helps regulate the hydrogen production pathways, and redirection of non-hydrogen favoring pathway via guiding the microbial metabolism towards increased synthesis of acetate and butyrate.

Previous studies demonstrated that modifying the carbon quantum dots with co-catalyst has the potential to increase the hydrogen generation capabilities in the presence of light-assisted catalyst via overcoming the electron-hole pairs and low electron transfer ability. As recently, overviewed by Wang et al., [16] modifying the quantum dots with a nitrogen-doped metallic interface improves the hydrogen production metabolism of the hydrogen producers due to higher surface area, improved electron transfer ability and direct supply of electrons as reducing power to hydrogen producers, which in turn diverts the metabolic flux via blocking of the hydrogen-consuming pathway such as propionic, lactic acid and stimulates the metabolic pathway towards the synthesis of acetate, butyrate and formate. Besides, the metallic-based nanomaterials possess higher toxicity to the hydrogen producers, whereas the non-metal-based carbon nanomaterials possess’ low cytotoxicity, high stability and low cost [2,17]. Furthermore, CQDs have wide applications in the field of energy, environment, bioimaging, and electrochemical applications, etc [18–25].

The objective of this study is to synthesize bio-compatible CQDs having a size range of <5 nm with hydrogen pathway inducer properties during dark fermentation reactions. Here, we reported a biohybrid system with organic interfaces to improve the dark fermentation system from Lactobacillus delbreuckii biocatalyst. The carbon quantum dots were prepared using microwave-assisted chemical -synthesis. It was revealed that augmentation of the various surface-charged carbon quantum dots promoted hydrogen production and the effect of their loadings on biohybrid systems was investigated. The efficacy of CQDs on hydrogen metabolic pathway and external electron transfer (EET) mechanism via soluble metabolic products i.e. VFA and cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis were also investigated. This study provides vital information on tailoring the biocatalyst with a desired concentration of biocompatible CQDs provides improved EET ability and metabolic flux regulator with tuneable hydrogen production.

The microbial strain used in this study (Lactobacillus delbrueckii NCIM 2365) was purchased from the NCIM culture collection centre in India. Microbial culture was cultivated in a nutrient-rich media (MRS broth) and then placed in an incubator shaker at 37°C for 24 h- at 100 rpm. Before the initiation of the hydrogen production experiments, an overnight-grown mid-log phase culture was used [10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, N-CQDs and its anchoring on Lactobacillus delbrueckii
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) synthesis was carried out through a microwave-assisted method. In a typical procedure, 0.57 g of L-Cystine and 1.5 g of Citric Acid were combined in a solution containing 10 mL of deionized water. To introduce nitrogen doping, 0.1 g of urea was added to the mixture. The resulting solution was stirred on a magnetic stirrer at 500 rpm for 1 h to ensure homogeneity. Following the stirring process, microwave heating was applied for 2 min, resulting in the observation of a distinct brownish colouration. Approximately 20 mL of additional deionized water was introduced into the solution, and UV-DRS (UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy) analysis was conducted. Notably, a peak in the range of 300–400 nm was observed, indicative of successful carbon quantum dot formation. To eliminate any suspended particles, present in the solution, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 5 min. This process resulted in a clear, brownish-transparent solution. The supernatant was carefully separated and filtered, and the purified solution was stored at room temperature. For un-doped CQD synthesis, all similar procedures were followed except the addition of urea during synthesis.

The synthesis of N-CQDs or CQDs anchored to Lactobacillus delbrueckii involved the following procedure: Lactobacillus delbrueckii was cultured and subsequently harvested. To this, 20 mL of N-CQDs was added to 2.3 g of centrifuged Lactobacillus delbrueckii. The resulting solution was vortexed for 20 minutes, allowed to stand for 3 h, and then cultured in MRS broth. After 72 h of cultivation, the product, either N-CQDs or CQD-Lactobacillus delbrueckii, could be obtained. Thereafter, the synthesized N-CQDs or CQD-Lactobacillus delbrueckii were used for biohydrogen production. TEM imaging of the CQDs and microbe was performed with an FEI TITAN 80–300 transmission electron microscope. The particle size of the CQDs and N-CQDs was analyzed by LS 13 320 XR Particle Size Analyzer (Beckman Coulter Life Sciences). The photoluminescent (PL) spectra of the samples were obtained using a photoluminescence setup (PerkinElmer Singapore PTE Ltd., Singapore, Model LS 55) with the samples dissolved in an aqueous solution. The crystal structure of the synthesized g-CQDs was analysed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Rigaku-D/MAX 2500 diffractometer. The measurements were conducted using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at a scanning speed of 4°/min over a 2θ range of 5° to 60°, with the sample drop cast onto a glass substrate for testing. The XPS spectra were obtained using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe II spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source. confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images of the Lactobacillus delbreuckii and its biohybrid system were performed with a Nikon AX Confocal microscope.

Biohydrogen fermentation experiments were conducted in a serum vial with a total volume of 125 mL, of which 50 mL served as the working volume. Minimal salt media with following ingredients 10 g/L glucose, 3 g/L NH4HCO3, 4.5 g/L NaHCO3, 0.125 g/L KH2PO4, 0.100 g/L MgCl2·6 H2O, 0.015 g/L MnSO4·6 H2O, 0.025 g/L FeSO4·7 H2O, and 0.005 g/L CuSO4·5 H2O dissolved in 1000 mL of deionized water were used. The vials were sealed tightly and purged with 99.99 % N2 gas for 2 min to establish anaerobic conditions. The vials were inoculated using a 10 % v/v inoculum, and the pH was adjusted to 6.5 before the addition of the inoculum. For organic-biohybrid preparation during BHP, the actively grown cells of Lactobacillus delbrueckii with 10 % v/v was used along with varied concentrations of the chemically synthesized CQDs and N-CQDs. The prepared hybrid systems were assessed for BHP for over 96 h of fermentation period and were placed in a shaking incubator at a consistent 37°C and 100 rpm. All the experiments were performed in duplicates and the data represented are the averages derived from the replicates.

Biogas was measured using a water displacement method by taking samples from the headspace of the serum vials at various time intervals. The composition analysis (H2, CO2) is analyzed with a gas chromatography system equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (GC-TCD, Nat GAS-B analyzer). Operational conditions of the GC-TCD are as follows; The column temperature was adjusted to 60°C, the injection port temperature was set to 100°C, and the TCD was operated at 110°C. Helium served as the carrier gas, flowing at a rate of 30 mL/min. soluble metabolic end products (SMPs) were analyzed using a high-performance liquid chromatography system (Agilent) equipped with a Hi-Plex-H column (300 mm × 7.7 mm, Agilent Corp) and a refractive index detector (Agilent). A mobile phase at a desired concentration of 5 mM H2SO4 was used. The HPLC was operated at a flow rate of 0.7 mL/min, while the column temperature was set at 60°C. The surface charge of the synthesized CQDs and microbe was measured using a zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Analytical) Fig. 1.

The modified Gompertz Eq. (1) was used to derive the biohydrogen kinetics data.
(1)
H
( t )
= P ⋅ exp
{
− exp
[
R
m
⋅ e
P
(
λ − t
)
+ 1
]
}

In this context, (P) signifies the maximum potential for hydrogen production in mL/L, and (Rm) represents the maximum rate of hydrogen production in mL/h. The symbol 'e' denotes the mathematical constant approximately equal to 2.71828. Furthermore, (λ) represents the lag phase of the culture, and 't' indicates the duration of the hydrogen production experiment in hours were calculated using Sigma plot 10.0 software.

Dehydrogenase activity in the bacterial culture was evaluated through the reduction of 2,3,5-triphenyl tetrazolium chloride (TTC), following a previously outlined method [26]. Briefly, 5 mL of the culture was pipetted into a sealed test tube, followed by the addition of 5 mL of TTC solution (prepared at 5 g/L) and 2 mL of 0.1 molar glucose. The mixture was thoroughly mixed using a vortex shaker for 20 minutes and then allowed to incubate at 37°C for 12 hours. After the incubation, 1 mL of concentrated H2SO4 solution was added to halt the deoxidation process, and 5 mL of toluene was introduced to extract the triphenyl formazan (TF) formed during the reaction. The contents were subsequently centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 minutes, and the resulting supernatant was measured at 492 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis aimed to evaluate alterations in the microbes' redox properties and their electron-holding capabilities after the introduction of CQDs and N-CQDs, in comparison to a control group. CV was conducted in a three-electrode system, working electrode- Graphite; counter electrode- Titanium, reference electrode- Ag/AgCl and operated at the scanning rate of 10 mv/s in the potential window of −0.8 V to 0.2 V under potentiostat-galvanostat mode (IviumStat, The Netherlands).

The Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) ( Fig. 2(a)) and N-CQD (Fig. 2(b)) represent a pivotal aspect of our investigation, offering a nanoscale perspective on their morphological characteristics. The TEM analysis of both CQD and N-CQD reveals well-defined, uniformly dispersed spherical particles with an average size of 5 nm. Notably, the images also capture the monodispersity of the CQDs, highlighting the precision achieved in their synthesis. The TEM images reveal the nanoscale arrangement of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the microbe, illustrating their uniform dispersion and potential interactions with cellular structures. The biohybrid material maintains a well-defined structure, indicating compatibility between the CQDs (Fig. 2-d) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) (Fig. 2c) with the biological components. This alignment of CQDs and N-CQDs on the overall surface of the microbe enhances electron transfer phenomena, resulting in a well-defined biohybrid. The TEM characterization also allows for the observation of any potential changes in the size, shape, or distribution of the CQDs upon integration with biological entities. Notably, the alignment of N-CQDs on the microbe is observed to be more pronounced compared to the CQD-microbe hybrid, suggesting an enhanced and well-aligned integration of N-CQDs with the microbial surface, potentially influencing the electron transfer dynamics more favourably. The UV image of N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) on the microbial surface highlights their intricate structures and quantum confinement effects (Fig-2 f). When exposed to UV light, the N-CQDs- Lactobacillus delbrueckii exhibit vivid fluorescence, a phenomenon known as photoluminescence. This effect enables the precise visualization and characterization of N-CQDs interacting with Lactobacillus delbrueckii. As shown in (Fig-2 g and h), Lactobacillus delbrueckii alone does not exhibit photoluminescence under UV light, whereas the N-CQDs display a distinct photoluminescence image. This indicates that the photoluminescence is specifically the inherent nature of N-CQDs.

The XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. 3a, revealed a prominent peak near 29.2°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite. This peak's broad nature suggests partial graphitization and indicates the small size of the synthesized CQDs. Similarly, the XRD analysis of N-CQDs, depicted in Fig. 3b, displayed a comparable pattern, pointing towards an amorphous carbon structure. The PL spectra of both CQDs and N-CQDs were recorded when excited at a wavelength of 285 nm, as shown in Fig. 3c and d. Upon examining the graph, it is evident that the N-CQDs exhibited a significantly stronger emission intensity compared to the CQDs, even though both had the same absorbance level. This enhanced emission suggests that the nitrogen doping in N-CQDs substantially improved their photoluminescent properties, resulting in a higher quantum yield than the undoped CQDs. The increased fluorescence efficiency of N-CQDs can be attributed to the introduction of nitrogen atoms, which may have created additional emissive sites or modified the electronic structure, thus enhancing the overall photoluminescent performance.

The XPS analysis of the as-synthesized CQDs and N-CQDs is illustrated in Fig. 4. The survey scan for the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4a, indicates that they are primarily composed of carbon and oxygen, corresponding to the C1s and O1s peaks, respectively. No other residual elements were detected, underscoring the high purity of the CQDs. In contrast, the survey scan of the N-CQDs reveals the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, reflecting the incorporation of nitrogen. The deconvolution of the C1s peak for the CQDs (Fig. 4b) shows a dominant peak at 282.9 eV, which corresponds to the sp2 graphitic structure, with additional peaks at 283.6 and 287.2 eV attributed to C-O and CO groups, respectively. For the N-CQDs, the deconvolution of the C1s peak reveals peaks at 287.01, 282.8, 283.1, and 284.01 eV, which are associated with carbon in the forms of CO, C–C/CC, C-N and C-O, respectively, indicating successful nitrogen functionalization.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to elucidate the structural characteristics and functional groups present in the synthesized materials ( Fig. 5). Specifically conducted for the microbial samples, the FTIR spectra shed light on the biomolecular composition, with phosphate stretching vibrations (PO₂⁻) due to nucleic acids being prominent around 1200–900 cm⁻¹ and the C-O stretching vibrations in ribose, a component of RNA, are observed around 1100–1000 cm⁻¹. Furthermore, the as-synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit a broad band around 2800 cm⁻¹, corresponding to O-H stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl groups on the surface. Additionally, peaks in the 3000 cm⁻¹ range signify C-H stretching vibrations, reflecting the presence of carbon-hydrogen bonds in organic moieties. C-O stretching vibrations observed in the 1000–1200 cm⁻¹ range further contribute to the characterization of surface functionalization. Following nitrogen functionalization (N-CQD), peaks around 1650–1750 cm⁻¹ corresponding to CO stretching vibrations, and those in the range of 1300–1600 cm⁻¹ associated with C-N stretching vibrations were identified. These spectral features provide valuable insights into the nitrogen doping-induced modifications on the surface of carbon quantum dots, facilitating a better understanding of their potential applications in various fields. After microbial integration on QDS and NQDS, peaks corresponding to microbes at 1200–900 cm⁻¹ (phosphate stretching vibrations) appear along with the inherent spectrum of QDS and NQDS, indicating the formation of microbe-QDS and microbe-NQDS biohybrid.

Fig. 6, showed the CLSM images of the bare bacteria and the prepared Lactobacillus delbreuckii does not show any luminescence properties in the scanning range of 405 nm to 561 nm, In the presence of CQDs, the luminescence showed the multi spectra signals at the wavelength of 405 and 488 nm, whereas the N-CQDs showed multi spectra signals at 405, 488, and 561 nm, inferred that modifications in the prepared N-CQDs influenced with a binding property of the bacterial cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biohydrogen production from CQDs assisted biohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The performance evaluation of un-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) Lactobacillus delbreuckii (organic-hybrid biocatalyst) with varied loadings (25 µL/L, 50 µL/L, 100 µL/L, and 200 µL/L) was assessed for biohydrogen production along with bare bacteria (Lactobacillus delbrecukii) and the biohydrogen production data was outlined in Fig. 7a and b. Fig. 7a demonstrates the variation in cumulative biohydrogen production from glucose upon augmentation of QDs and bare bacteria without QDs. Without the QDs addition, the cumulative hydrogen production reached a value of 2144 mL/L after 72 h of the glucose fermentation. The notable increment in cumulative hydrogen production was observed by the augmentation of QDs into the fermentation medium. The augmentation of CQDs in the order of 25, 50, 100 and 200 µL/L provided the cumulative hydrogen production (CHP) of 2266 ± 95, 2383± 51, 2584 ± 95, and 2193 ± 60 mL/L. with hydrogen yield (HY) of 1.63 ± 0.07, 1.71 ± 0.04, 1.86± 0.07, and 1.58 ± 0.04 mol/mol hexose, respectively. The augmentation of the organic interface (CQDs) with biohydrogen-producing bacteria (Lactobacillus delbrecukii) resulted in great improvement in both CHP and HY. The notable increment in HY of 1.86± 0.07 mol/mol hexose was attained with 100 µL/L augmentation CQDs, however, when the concentration of CQDs >100 µL/L does not greatly favour the hydrogen production profile of the Lactobacillus delbrecukii – hybrid system and resulted in the least HY of 1.58 ± 0.04 mol/mol hexose amongst the organic-biohybrid system, which is similar to the non-QDs added bacteria.

Table 1 shows the kinetic parameters of the biohydrogen production simulated with the experimental data. The kinetic values are statistically significant with an adjusted R2 square value of > 0.97 showing a good-fit curve of the experimental data. The maximum cumulative hydrogen production potential (CHP) and hydrogen production rate (HPR) were noted in the range of 2098 ± 56–2541 ± 100 mL/L and 1399–2050 mL/L/d. The hydrogen production was improved in the presence of 25 and 200 µL/L of the CQDs, however, the corresponding HPR value was not significantly improved, and it negatively affected the HPR. At 50 µL/L augmentation, the CHP and HPR were slightly improved. Under the optimal conditions of 100 µL/L of CQDs augmentation, the peak HPR value of 2050 mL/L/d was attained, which is about 30 % higher than the bare bacteria without CQDs. The observed difference might be attributed to the change in the lag phase value, in which at 50 and 100 µL/L concentration the lag phase value was shorter, in which the interaction of CQDs with LAB is faster, whereas at 25 and 200 µL/L dosage, the lag phase value of 3.5 and 3.7 h was observed, which is like the non-CQDs supplementation value of 3.8 h. The HY also slightly improved at 25 and 200 µL/L dosage. Similar, hydrogen kinetics data were attained with glucose fermentation during supplementation of TiO2 and Fe3O4 nanoparticles, in which varied concentrations of nanoparticles showed diverse hydrogen production kinetics [27,28].

Fig. 7b shows the profile of the soluble metabolic products (SMP) analysis collected after the end of the fermentation. The microbial metabolism shows a distinct pattern upon the addition of CQDs. Lactobacillus delbrecukii follows a mixed –acid type fermentation dominated by butyrate, acetate, and formate and the least production of lactate and propionate in native form as well during the augmentation of CQDs. During the supplementation of CQDs, the proportion of acetic and butyric acid concentrations varied with different loading of the CQDs with Lactobacillus delbrecukii. Without CQDs Lactobacillus delbrecukii produced a SMP of lactate, formate, acetate, propionate and butyrate concentration of 0.29, 0.69, 2.92, 0.28, 2.18 g/L. The optimized concentration of 100 µL/L of QDs addition, the concentration of acetate, butyrate, lactate, and format were noted as 3.24, 2.58, 0.54, and 0.63 g/L respectively. Acetate and butyrate account for over 85 % of the total SMP profile. The lactate concentration remains less than 0.5 g/L in all cases of the CQDs augmentation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biohydrogen production from nitrogen doped N-QDs biohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further enhance hydrogen production, we have modified the carbon quantum dots with nitrogen pair (urea) to improve the stability of the catalyst with higher electron transfer ability. The produced N-CQDs have a zeta potential value of + 2. 5 mv, which provides an active binding to the negatively charged bacterial surface of the LAB (-17 mv). The hydrogen production ability of the N-CQDs with Lactobacillus delbrecukii hybrid system was studied at varied dosages of 25, 50, 100, and 200 µL/L and their hydrogen production profile was illustrated in Fig. 8 and b. Fig. 8a shows a varied hydrogen production profile in the presence of N-CQDs augmented Lactobacillus delbrecukii. Augmentation of N-CQDs at a concentration of 25, 50, 100, and 200 µL/L provided a hydrogen production of 2615 ± 69, 2651± 51, 2792 ± 129, and 2315 ± 95 mL/L, which are higher than the bacteria without N-CQDs (2144 mL/L). Addition of N-CQDs at a least concentration of 25 µL/L also improves overall HY than the CQDs supplementation at 100 µL/L and reached a value of 1.88 ± 0.05 mol/mol hexose. With N-QDs augmentation, the HY was observed in the range of 1.67–2.01 ± 0.09 mol/mol hexose. The maximum HY of 2.01 ± 0.09 mol/mol hexose was attained with N-QDs blend of 100 µL/L with Lactobacillus delbrecukii. For instance, Xiao et al. [29] studied the electrostatic interaction of charge differenced non-metallic Quantum dots with modified Escherichia coli system and attained improved hydrogen production ability due to the efficient electron distribution with organic- biohybrid system. The biohybrid system performance was enhanced up to 9 % and achieved via the involvement of NADH/NAD+ in indirectly transferring intracellular photoelectrons to formate dehydrogenase. In this study, about 45 % enhancement in hydrogen production was noticed under optimal conditions (100 µL/L) of N-CQDs- biohybrid catalyst.

Table 2 shows the key kinetic parameters of the biohydrogen production with N-CQDs biohybrid system at varied concentrations. The CHP and HPR were greatly enhanced with the augmentation of N-CQDs into the fermentation medium. The maximum CHP and HPR of 2792 ± 129 mL/L and 2412± 60 mL/L/d were obtained with 100 µL/L of N-CQDs addition. Due to the charge difference between the N-CQDs and bacteria the interaction and affinity of microbes- N-CQDs catalyst improves the hydrogen production, hydrogen yield and production rate. With the presence of N-CQDs the HPR production rate was enhanced and observed in the range of 1902–2412 mL/L/d, which is about 19–35 % higher than the LAB without N-CQDs addition. The lag phase of the biohybrid system is shorter in comparison with LAB alone. The shorter lag phase shows better adaptability and biocompatibility of the synthesized N-CQDs with Lactobacillus delbrecukii hybrid system, which in turn improves the biohydrogen production kinetics and yield. Similar biohydrogen production kinetics was attained with thermophilic mixed-culture system, while using green synthesised magnetic iron nanoparticle synergistically enhanced the biohydrogen production kinetic with reduction in lag-phase duration [27].

Fig. 8b shows the SMP production of the N-CQDs biohybrid system. Like the role of CQDs, the addition of N-CQDs significantly induced the production of acetic and butyric acid as a major metabolite, with less formation of lactic and propionic acid. The proportion of acetate, butyrate, and formate increases as the concentration of N-CQDs increases. The acetic acid concentration was observed in the range of 2.66–3.02 g/L, whereas butyrate concentration was noted as 2.22–2.61 g/L, notably the formate concentration was observed in the range of 0.92–1.12 g/L. Both lactic and propionic acid concentration was maintained less than 0.3 g/L in all the tested concentrations. The addition of CQDs/N-CQDs into the fermentation medium actively uptake by the bacterial metabolism and it diverts the metabolic flux from lactate and propionate (hydrogen consuming) to acetate, butyrate and formate (hydrogen generating) mediated hydrogen metabolism. The generation of hydrogen-favorable SMPs i.e. acetate and butyrate during fermentation is linked with the inherent metabolism of hydrogen producers, upon which the maximum hydrogen yield can be attained when these two metabolites are dominant in the overall SMPs profile [30–32]. In this study, the Lactobacillus delbrecukii follows a mixed –acid type fermentation dominated by butyrate, acetate, formate and the least production of lactate and propionate in native form as well during the augmentation of CQDs.

The obtained HY from this study was similar to the earlier findings of various hydrogen producing bacteria dominated by Lactobacillus sp ( Table 3). In recent findings, the co-occurrence of LAB with hydrogen producers is known and their co-establishment improves the hydrogen production pathway via the internal/externally induced LAB-mediated hydrogen production pathway. It is widely believed that the co-occurrence of LAB is hindering the hydrogen production metabolism due to the diversion of reducing energy for the synthesis of lactic acid. In recent studies, the appearance of LAB with dominant hydrogen producers favours the hydrogen production metabolism, as it acts as a reducing agent in the form of removing the dissolved oxygen, and provides the lactate- butyrate mediated hydrogen production pathway to generate additional hydrogen from the feedstock [31,33–36]. For instance, Rasmey et al. [32] attained a HY of 1571 mL/L from molasses via co-culturing of Lactobacillus sp with Clostridium sp. Ohnishi et al. [37] reported a HY of 1.27 mol/mol hexose from co-culturing of Clostridium butyricum with Lactobacillus delbrecukii. Kim et al. [38] obtained an HY of 1.67 mol/mol hexose from Clostridium butyricum via the addition of lactate into the fermentation medium. In this study, the apparent improvement in HY via augmentation of CQDs/ N-CQDs is the result of induced microbial metabolism with improved hydrogenase activity, and good electron transfer ability of the prepared biohybrid system, which will be discussed below in sub-sections.

Dehydrogenase (DH) enzymes are significant contributors to hydrogen fermentation, facilitating the conversion of electrons produced during substrate oxidation under anaerobic conditions. The dehydrogenase enzyme plays a governing role in transferring protons (H+) between the metabolites via shuffling the redox mediators like NAD+ and FAD+ and channelizing the hydrogen and SMP formation [39]. This transfer of H+ ions between metabolic pathways accelerates electron flow, leading to increased hydrogen generation. The dehydrogenase activity of Lactobacillus delbrueckii cells obtained after 48 hours of fermentation was observed in the presence of both carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and the results are summarized in Fig. 9 and b. The dehydrogenase enzyme activity of Lactobacillus delbrueckii was 11.55 μg/mL, with the presence of CQDs and N-CQDS the activity of DH was noted in the range of 13.51–26.49 μg/mL and 14.78–31.34 μg/mL. The activity of DH is directly co-related with the improved HY attained by the organic-biohybrid interface. The highest recorded DH activity was observed at 100 µL/L of N-CQDs, reaching 31.34 μg/mL, followed by 26.49 μg/mL at the same concentration of CQDs. Similar hydrogenase activity enhancement was noted in the presence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles during thermophilic fermentation of Parageobacillus thermoglucosidasius [40]. The improved DH activity followed by the augmentation of CQDs and N-CQDs suggests a redirection of metabolic flux, leading to improved hydrogen production.

A cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis of the hydrogen producing bacteria with the presence of nanomaterials provides useful information about the dynamics of the external electron transfer (EET) mechanism [41,42]. The CV curve obtained from LAB under the presence of CQDs and N-CQDs showed a sigmoidal shape from −0.8 V to 0.2 V ( Fig. 10 a). Compared to the Lactobacillus delbrueckii, the current generation was higher with the presence of CQDs and N-CQDS, suggesting that the biohybrid systems actively participated in the electron transfer during hydrogen fermentation. The key hydrogen-producing redox pairs NAD+/NADH (E0 – 0.32 V) and FAD+/FADH2 (E0 −0.24 V) activity was improved after the augmentation of the carbon quantum dots. N-CQDs addition improved the activity of both redox-pairs (NAD+/NADH and FAD+/FADH2) and reached over 27 % activity than the Lactobacillus delbrueckii alone (Fig. 10 b). The CQDs addition leads to an improvement of over ∼12 %. Similar changes in the charge transfer of redox pairs were noted during hydrogen fermentation [38,42] and it inferred that the augmentation of carbon quantum dots perturbate the microbial metabolism via redirecting the metabolic flux towards the synthesis of hydrogen-producing metabolic pathway, rather than the hydrogen consuming pathway and also actively participated in the EET mechanism during hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for the enhancement of hydrogen production by N- CQDs augmentation
ce:section.ce:para:
Facultative hydrogen production metabolism is mediated via PFL and NADH pathway, in PFL pathway during glycolysis the pyruvate is transformed into acetyl-COA, (a precursor of acetic acid and butyric acid synthesis) and formate. Formate is reacted with formate hydrogen lyase (FHL) complex to liberate hydrogen and carbon dioxide (Eq. 2). In NADH pathway, NADH acts as a precursor molecule for the synthesis of hydrogen via ferrodoxin (Fd)-NAD+ reductase and ferredoxin hydrogenase (Eq. 3). Perturbation of FHL pathway is one of the significant ways to improve the NADH mediated hydrogen production. Since, the production of lactate is energy consuming reaction, which is closely associated with FHL pathway [43]. During the homo-lactic fermentation of LAB in microaerophilic conditions, the pyruvate is reduced into lactate via utilizing the reducing power of NADH generated during glycolysis and yielded 2 moles of lactic acid from one of glucose (Eq. 4) [44].
(2) HCOOH →H2+ CO2
(3) NADH + H+ →NAD+ + H2
(4) Pyruvate+ NADH ↔ Lactic acid + NAD+

Based on the findings of this study, it was concluded that augmentation of N-CQDs at a desired concentration of 100 µL/L stimulated both the hydrogen production rate and yield from glucose. The enhancement in HPR and HY is attributed to the improved hydrogenase activity and external electron transfer of Lactobacillus delbreuckii. The SMPs analysis showed that augmentation of CQDs redirects the metabolic flux towards the hydrogen synthesis pathway with the generation of enriched concentration of acetate and butyrate and diminished concentration of lactate. Augmentation of N-CQDs influences the microbial metabolism favoured via improved hydrogenase activity and redirection of redox-pairs NAD+/NADH and FAD+/FADH2 towards higher hydrogen production with the least production of lactate. Lactic acid accumulation consumes 1 mol of NADH, whereas acetate accumulation does not consume NADH, Hence, changes in the metabolic pathway allow the efficient utilization of NADH in converting H+ ions into H2, resulting in accelerated hydrogen production.

This study demonstrates the use of carbon quantum dots promotes the biohydrogen production with Lactobacillus delbreuckii stain in dark fermentation. In summary, we have developed a bio-compatible CQDs with a fine particle size of < 5 nm and developed an efficient organic-bio hybrid system. TEM analysis showed that augmented cQDS inferred with outer surface and intracellular cell structure of the Lactobacillus delbreuckii biohybrid system and maintained a well-defined structure, which improved the electron transfer and hydrogenase activity of the hybrid system, with improved hydrogen production dynamics. Consequently, the biohybrid system demonstrated a significant 18–33-fold increase in hydrogen yield in comparison to bacteria without CQDs augmentation. This study provides novel perspectives on constructing organic-biohybrid systems and offering tuneable hydrogen production via dark-fermentation.

Umish Srivastva: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Ravi Prkash Gupta: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Debasis Bhattacharyya: Validation, Conceptualization. P Sivagurunathan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Manoj Kumar Upreti: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Prakash C Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metabolic engineering approaches for scale-up of fermentative biohydrogen production–A review

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticle-associated single step hydrogen fermentation for the conversion of starch potato waste biomass by thermophilic Parageobacillus thermoglucosidasius

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Singhvi, A. Maharjan, A. Thapa, H.-B. Jun, B.S. Kim, Nanoparticle-associated single step hydrogen fermentation for the conversion of starch potato waste biomass by thermophilic Parageobacillus thermoglucosidasius, Bioresour Technol 337 (2021) 125490.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Municipal landfill leachate remediation coupling acidogenesis and bioelectrogenesis for biohydrogen and volatile fatty acids production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Yellappa, O. Sarkar, Y.V.R. Reddy, S.V. Mohan, Municipal landfill leachate remediation coupling acidogenesis and bioelectrogenesis for biohydrogen and volatile fatty acids production, Process Safety and Environmental Protection 172 (2023) 716–726. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psep.2023.02.027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Performance and mechanism of different pretreatment methods for inoculated sludge in biohydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Cheng, L. Wang, Y. Xu, S. Li, Q. Wang, R. Chen, L. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perturbation of formate pathway and NADH pathway acting on the biohydrogen production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Liu, Y. Sun, Y. Li, Y. Lu, Perturbation of formate pathway and NADH pathway acting on the biohydrogen production, Sci Rep 7 (2017) 9587.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-titer lactic acid production from pilot-scale pretreated non-detoxified rice straw hydrolysate at high-solid loading

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Sivagurunathan, T. Raj, P.S. Chauhan, P. Kumari, A. Satlewal, R.P. Gupta, R.
